Execute any of these with: invoke_tool({ name: "<tool>", target: "edgesynology1|edgesynology2|both", args: { ... } })

TOOL: check_drive_database
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Checks Synology Drive's internal SQLite databases for corruption. Database corruption causes persistent sync failures that won't resolve on their own. Searches across all data volumes.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_drive_package_health
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Checks whether the Synology Drive package is properly installed — its status, version, internal database files, and log locations. Detects broken installations. Searches across all data volumes.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_synology_drive_network
GROUP: network
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Checks Synology Drive network state — whether port 6690 is listening, active sync connections by client, recent network errors in the Drive log, and whether the Drive config shows a non-default bind address.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: list_drive_version_history
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Queries the Synology Drive SQLite database for version history of a specific file. Requires exact_path. Shows available version tables and attempts to list versions by filename.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  exact_path: string  // Exact absolute filesystem path to inspect (e.g. /volume1/data/folder/file.txt).

TOOL: repair_drive_db_permissions
GROUP: write_files
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Fixes ownership and permissions on @synologydrive database directories across all volumes. Useful when Synology Drive fails to start due to permission errors on its database files.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: restart_synology_drive_server
GROUP: write_restart
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Restarts the Synology Drive package via DSM WebAPI. Use when Drive is unresponsive or in an error state. Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in the container .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: restart_synology_drive_sharesync
GROUP: write_restart
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Restarts the SynologyDriveShareSync package via DSM WebAPI. Use when ShareSync is stuck or not syncing. Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in the container .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: search_drive_path_activity
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Searches Synology Drive logs and ShareSync logs for activity related to a path, filename, or username. Use to investigate sync events, file changes, or errors for a specific file. Pass the search term in filter.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: search_drive_server_log
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Searches the Synology Drive server log for a specific word or phrase — such as a share name, username, or error message.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: tail_drive_server_log
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows the most recent entries from the Synology Drive server log.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.

TOOL: check_daemon_processes
GROUP: packages
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows process state and resource usage for key Synology daemons: synologand, invoked, syncd, cloud-control, syno_drive_server, synoindex, and crond. Use to identify a crashed or resource-heavy daemon.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_disk_error_trends
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows SMART error-relevant attributes (reallocated sectors, pending sectors, uncorrectable errors, temperature, power-on hours) for all disks in a compact table. Use to spot a drive silently accumulating errors.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_kernel_io_errors
GROUP: logs
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Reads the Linux kernel error log for disk errors, SCSI faults, filesystem corruption, and memory problems. Critical for distinguishing a software bug from a failing hard drive.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_package_runtime
GROUP: packages
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Checks a DSM package's runtime state: synopkg status, enabled flag, var directory contents, PID files, lock files, and matching processes. Pass the package name exactly as shown by synopkg (e.g. SynologyDrive, HyperBackup).
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  package_name: string  // Synology package name exactly as shown by synopkg (e.g. SynologyDrive, HyperBackup, ContainerManager).

TOOL: check_service_ports
GROUP: network
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows listener state and active connection count for each key Synology service port: SMB (445), NFS (2049), SSH (22), DSM (5000/5001), Drive sync (6690), rsync (873), and LDAP (389/636).
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_share_database
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Lists all shared folders from the DSM share database. Failures here indicate database corruption which prevents Synology Drive from working.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_volume_health
GROUP: system
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Checks storage pool and volume health at the DSM layer (synovolumestatus, synoarraystatus), /proc/mdstat for RAID state, per-volume disk usage, and SMART overall health for all drives.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: clear_package_lockfiles
GROUP: write_files
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Removes stale lock files for a named DSM package that are preventing it from starting or updating. Pass the package name in filter (e.g. 'SynologyDrive'). Lists all lock files found before removing them.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: collect_incident_bundle
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Collects a targeted set of diagnostics for a specific incident type. Pass the type in filter: 'drive' (Drive/ShareSync), 'storage' (RAID/SMART/Btrfs), 'network' (interfaces/DNS), 'permission' (ACL/share), or 'crash' (daemon/OOM/segfault). Omit filter for general system snapshot.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: fetch_package_db
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Queries a named DSM package's SQLite database. Pass the package name and optionally a SQL query in filter. Omit filter to list all tables and row counts. Use for deep investigation of Drive, HyperBackup, or scheduler internal state.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  package_name: string  // Synology package name exactly as shown by synopkg (e.g. SynologyDrive, HyperBackup, ContainerManager).
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: find_path_versions_and_snapshots
GROUP: files
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Looks for recoverable versions of a path: Btrfs snapshots under @Recently-Snapshot, recycle bin entries, and any Drive version hints in logs. Requires exact_path pointing to a file or directory.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  exact_path: string  // Exact absolute filesystem path to inspect (e.g. /volume1/data/folder/file.txt).

TOOL: get_resource_snapshot
GROUP: performance
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Full live picture of the NAS: top processes by CPU/memory, disk I/O per disk, active SMB/NFS/Drive connections, ShareSync backlog, memory pressure, and recent kernel errors. Best first tool to run when diagnosing any performance problem.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: tail_package_logs
GROUP: logs
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows recent log entries for a specific DSM package. Checks the standard log locations under /var/packages and /var/log/synolog. Pass the package name exactly as shown by synopkg (e.g. SynologyDrive, HyperBackup).
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  package_name: string  // Synology package name exactly as shown by synopkg (e.g. SynologyDrive, HyperBackup, ContainerManager).
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.

TOOL: trigger_sharesync_resync
GROUP: write_restart
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Forces a ShareSync re-sync by restarting the SynologyDriveShareSync package via DSM WebAPI. Specify the folder or task name in the 'filter' parameter (informational only — all ShareSync tasks resume on restart). Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in the container .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: check_sharesync_status
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Looks specifically for stuck, conflicted, or erroring ShareSync tasks in the recent logs. Searches across all data volumes.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.

TOOL: search_webapi_log
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Searches DSM WebAPI logs for share access failures, authentication errors, and API call failures.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: tail_sharesync_log
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows the most recent ShareSync log entries — what files are being synced and any sync errors. Searches across all data volumes.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.

payload.message: I’m doing one last pass on cause and risk: scheduled tasks/snapshot-related maintenance, Btrfs counters, SMART error trends, and Drive/ShareSync health. If the disks are clean, this is mostly a scheduling/workload problem; if SMART or kernel I/O errors show up, it becomes a hardware-risk conversation.

payload.message:
I checked both Synology units via the `synology-monitor` MCP.

**Bottom line:** `edgesynology2` is the real hot spot. The high iowait is coming from Btrfs snapshot cleanup / delayed-reference work on top of RAID5, not from ordinary user CPU load. `edgesynology1` is comparatively calm right now.

**What I found**

`edgesynology1`:
- Current iowait is low: `vmstat wa=0%`, sampled `/proc/stat` about `0.13%`.
- No D-state processes from the deeper process I/O probe.
- It did have recent RAID maintenance: kernel log shows `md4` and `md5` requested-resync activity earlier, plus some Btrfs snapshot drops on `dm-4`.
- Btrfs device error counters are clean: `write/read/flush/corruption/generation = 0`.

`edgesynology2`:
- Initial `top` showed iowait around `18%`, though later sampled iowait dipped, so this is bursty.
- Load was elevated: about `4.36`.
- Multiple D-state kernel workers were stuck in Btrfs/RAID paths:
  - `btrfs_tree_lock`
  - `wait_on_page_bit`
  - `btrfs_submit_bio_hook`
  - `wait_current_trans`
  - `raid5_get_active_stripe`
- Memory dirty/writeback was high: roughly `2.5 GB Dirty` and `74 MB Writeback`.
- Disk queue pressure was visible on md devices, especially `md4` earlier with `inprog: 145`.
- Kernel logs show repeated Btrfs snapshot deletion/reclaim jobs on `dm-2`, including large reclaim events. These are happening around hourly marks, for example `17:03`, `18:02`, `19:03`.
- Btrfs device stats show `corruption_errs = 25` on `/dev/mapper/cachedev_0`. That deserves attention even though the kernel log did not show obvious current disk I/O errors.
- RAID arrays are not degraded at the main data-array level, but system RAID1 arrays `md0` and `md1` show `[6/5] [UUUUU_]` on `edgesynology2`, which is odd and worth checking in DSM Storage Manager.

**Likely Cause**

The iowait spikes are most likely caused by scheduled snapshot deletion/retention cleanup on `edgesynology2`. Btrfs is doing heavy metadata cleanup and delayed-ref processing, then flushing that through RAID5. Synology Drive/Finder are active too, but the stack traces point below those services into Btrfs transaction/writeback machinery.

**Recommended Next Moves**

1. In DSM, inspect Snapshot Replication / shared-folder snapshot retention on `edgesynology2`, especially jobs running hourly around `:03`.
2. Stagger or move snapshot pruning to a quieter window. Avoid having snapshot deletion, indexing, Drive/ShareSync, backups, and scrubs overlap.
3. Check Storage Manager for `md0/md1` system partition health because `[6/5] [UUUUU_]` suggests one expected member is absent from those small RAID1 arrays.
4. Treat `Btrfs corruption_errs=25` as a caution flag: confirm backups are current, then schedule a Btrfs scrub/SMART review during off-hours.
5. Avoid restarting Drive/ShareSync as a first response. This looks kernel/filesystem-bound, and restarting app daemons may add more churn.

A couple of the packaged MCP probes were blocked by the NAS API tier, notably scheduled task inspection and some scheduler/PSI checks, but the process stacks and kernel logs were enough to identify the main pattern.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

content.text:
"inspect Snapshot Replication" - how does one  inspect Snapshot Replication? what am i looking for?
where do i go to Stagger or move snapshot pruning to a quieter window?
md0/md1 system partition - this unit never had 6 drives installed in it. how do we fix that?
the system does data scrubbing every month. the latest run finished 24 hours ago.
give me more detail about the MCP probes that were blocked by the NAS API tier. i'll have a developer fix it

payload.message:
"inspect Snapshot Replication" - how does one  inspect Snapshot Replication? what am i looking for?
where do i go to Stagger or move snapshot pruning to a quieter window?
md0/md1 system partition - this unit never had 6 drives installed in it. how do we fix that?
the system does data scrubbing every month. the latest run finished 24 hours ago.
give me more detail about the MCP probes that were blocked by the NAS API tier. i'll have a developer fix it

Here’s the practical version.

**Snapshot Replication**
In DSM: **Main Menu → Snapshot Replication → Snapshots**.

Look for:
- Shared folders with very frequent snapshots.
- Retention rules that expire/delete a lot at once.
- Schedules clustered at the same time, especially around `:03` past the hour. Your logs showed pruning at `17:03`, `18:02`, `19:03`.
- “Locked” or replicated snapshots piling up.
- Any replication task that runs near snapshot cleanup, Drive indexing, Hyper Backup, or monthly scrub.

To stagger/move pruning, go to **Snapshot Replication → Snapshots → Settings** for each protected shared folder. Check:
- Snapshot schedule.
- Retention policy.
- Advanced retention or smart recycle settings.

If multiple shares snapshot hourly, offset them: e.g. `files` at `:05`, `mac` at `:20`, `Photography` at `:35`, `users` at `:50`. The goal is to avoid Btrfs deleting many snapshots from many shares at once. For big shares, fewer snapshots or a less aggressive retention rule may help more than just offsetting.

**md0/md1**
`md0` and `md1` are DSM’s small system RAID arrays, not your main data pool. The output:

```text
md0 [6/5] [UUUUU_]
md1 [6/5] [UUUUU_]
```

means DSM metadata expects 6 members, but only 5 are present. If the unit truly never had 6 drives, this is probably stale/odd metadata or a chassis/model expectation, not necessarily the direct cause of the iowait.

Supported path:
- DSM → **Storage Manager → HDD/SSD**
- Check whether any disk says **System Partition Failed** or offers **Repair System Partition**.
- If DSM offers repair, use that.
- If DSM does not show a warning, I would not force a CLI mdadm repair just to make `[6/5]` pretty. Shrinking Synology system arrays manually is risky and unsupported.

The monthly scrub finishing 24 hours ago is useful context. The current traces did not look like active scrub; they looked like Btrfs snapshot deletion/cleanup. Scrub may have added background load recently, but the hourly Btrfs drop-snapshot messages are the stronger match.

**Blocked MCP Probes**
These were blocked by the NAS API validator:

- `check_io_stalls`
- `check_io_scheduler`
- `check_psi_pressure`
- `check_storage_pool_detail`
- `check_scrub_status`
- `check_scheduled_tasks`

Most returned:

```text
HTTP 403 — command requires tier 2 or higher (detected write pattern)
```

`check_scrub_status` returned:

```text
HTTP 403 — docker read command is not in the allowlist
```

Also, plain read-only compound shell probes were rejected, including commands using `awk`, `egrep`, `sleep`, `ps`, `lsblk`, `/proc/diskstats`, etc., with:

```text
This command requires write access and cannot be run via run_command.
```

For the developer: the validator is over-classifying read-only diagnostics as write-tier. These tools should either be allowlisted explicitly or split into simpler read-only primitives. The important one to fix first is `check_scheduled_tasks`, because that would confirm exactly which DSM task is pruning snapshots around `:03`.

payload.message:
Yes, some of Snapshot Replication is visible from the backend, but not cleanly with the current MCP permissions.

I tried just now:
- `tail_package_logs ReplicationService` worked, but it mostly showed package start/stop history, not the active snapshot schedule.
- `fetch_package_db ReplicationService` found no useful DB tables.
- `list_snapshot_candidates` was blocked as “tier 2 / detected write pattern,” even though conceptually it should just list snapshots.
- `check_package_runtime ReplicationService` was also blocked.

So: backend inspection is possible, but the current MCP server is blocking some of the exact read-only paths we would need.

For Snapshot Replication, the backend should ideally expose:
- Snapshot schedules per shared folder.
- Retention rules per shared folder.
- Replication tasks, if any.
- Snapshot count per share.
- Recent snapshot delete/prune events.

The most useful MCP tool to add/fix would be something like `inspect_snapshot_replication`, read-only, that gathers those four things.

On `md0/md1`: your DS1621xs+ is a 6-bay model, so `[6/5] [UUUUU_]` likely just means DSM created system RAID metadata expecting up to 6 bays, and only 5 disks are currently members. If the unit never had a 6th disk and DSM does not show “System Partition Failed,” there may be nothing to repair. “Repair System Partition” usually appears only when DSM detects a failed/missing system partition on an installed disk. The “fix” would generally be adding a 6th drive and letting DSM initialize/repair the system partition onto it. I would not try to shrink or rewrite `md0/md1` manually.

**Safe MCP Probes**
These blocked probes are definitely low-risk if implemented as narrow read-only allowlisted tools, not as broad tier escalation:

- `check_psi_pressure`
  - Reads `/proc/pressure/*`.
  - Pure read-only. Very safe.

- `check_io_stalls`
  - Reads `ps`, `/proc/*/wchan`, `/proc/*/stack`, `/proc/mdstat`, `dmesg`.
  - Safe if it does not attach debuggers or run `strace`.

- `check_scrub_status`
  - Reads `/proc/mdstat` and `btrfs scrub status`.
  - Safe. Do not include `btrfs scrub start/cancel`.

- `check_io_scheduler`
  - Reads `/sys/block/*/queue/scheduler`, queue settings, maybe `iostat -x`.
  - Safe if it never writes to `/sys/block/.../scheduler`.

- `check_storage_pool_detail`
  - Reads `mdadm --detail`, `synoarraystatus`, `synovolumestatus`.
  - Low-risk read-only.

- `check_scheduled_tasks`
  - Safe if implemented as schedule/list/log inspection only.
  - Important: do not reuse the same code path as `run_scheduled_task`, `enable_scheduled_task`, or `disable_scheduled_task`.

Also safe and worth unblocking:
- `list_snapshot_candidates`
- `check_package_runtime`
- `fetch_package_db` with SQLite opened read-only, ideally `mode=ro`
- a new `inspect_snapshot_replication` tool

The key developer guidance: don’t raise the whole NAS API to “tier 2.” Add exact command/tool allowlists for read-only diagnostics. The risky tools are the ones that mutate state: `set_io_scheduler`, `set_vm_dirty_ratios`, `cancel_btrfs_scrub`, `start_btrfs_scrub`, `restart_*`, `disable_scheduled_task`, `run_scheduled_task`, etc.

I rechecked, but we’re not reaching the expanded-permissions layer yet.

Every Synology Monitor MCP call now fails with:

```text
edgesynology1: connect ECONNREFUSED 100.107.131.35:7734
edgesynology2: connect ECONNREFUSED 100.107.131.36:7734
```

I tried both the previously blocked probes and simple baseline calls:

- `check_io_stalls`
- `check_io_scheduler`
- `check_psi_pressure`
- `check_storage_pool_detail`
- `check_scrub_status`
- `check_scheduled_tasks`
- `check_agent_container`
- `check_disk_space`
- `run_command: uptime`
- `restart_nas_api` preview

All hit the same `ECONNREFUSED`, so this is not an allowlist/permission failure anymore. It means the NAS-side API service on port `7734` is not listening, is crashed, or is blocked from the MCP host.

For the developer: first fix/restart the `synology-monitor-nas-api` service/container on both NAS units, or verify Tailscale/firewall reachability to `100.107.131.35:7734` and `100.107.131.36:7734`. Once that endpoint is listening again, I can rerun the expanded-permission probes and tell you exactly which ones are now unblocked.

payload.message:
Tried again, and the NAS API is back. The expanded access mostly worked.

What now works:
- `check_io_stalls`: works.
- `check_io_scheduler`: works.
- `check_psi_pressure`: runs, but PSI is unavailable on these DSM kernels/mounts.
- `check_storage_pool_detail`: runs, though some binaries are missing inside the agent environment.
- `check_scrub_status`: works.
- `check_scheduled_tasks`: runs, but looks in the wrong/missing DB path.
- `list_snapshot_candidates`: now works, but did not find visible snapshot candidate dirs from its current search path.
- `check_package_runtime ReplicationService`: works.
- `fetch_package_db ReplicationService`: works, but found no DB files/tables.

Current state is calm:
- `edgesynology1`: load `0.75, 0.81, 0.81`; no D-state I/O waiters; RAID idle.
- `edgesynology2`: load `0.27, 0.48, 0.62`; no D-state I/O waiters; disk queues at `0`; RAID idle.
- Scrub status on both says `Rate: 0.00B/s` and `Error summary: no errors found`.
- `edgesynology2` still shows `md0/md1 [6/5] [UUUUU_]`, which matches the 5 installed disks on a 6-bay unit.

Still needs developer cleanup:
- `check_scheduled_tasks` is looking for `/host/usr/syno/etc/schedule/synoscheduler.db`, but it reports that DB is not found. It needs updated DSM 7 path discovery.
- `check_storage_pool_detail` tries `synoarraystatus`, `synovolumestatus`, and `mdadm`, but those are unavailable in the agent path/container. It should locate DSM binaries on host paths or gracefully fall back to `/proc/mdstat` plus `smartctl`.
- Snapshot schedule/retention is still not exposed. `ReplicationService` package is enabled and `synobtrfsreplicad` is running, but its package DB/logs did not reveal schedule rules.

So the access expansion helped. The next useful MCP improvement is a dedicated read-only `inspect_snapshot_replication` tool that discovers DSM’s actual snapshot/retention config paths instead of relying on generic snapshot candidate listing.

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.message:
Yes, better now. A few probes were clearly improved.

`check_scheduled_tasks` now finds the DSM 7 scheduler DB on `edgesynology1` at:

```text
/host/usr/syno/etc/esynoscheduler/esynoscheduler.db
```

On `edgesynology2`, it still does **not** find the scheduler DB. It only checked:

```text
/host/etc
```

So the developer fixed discovery for one NAS path, but `edgesynology2` still needs host path coverage similar to `edgesynology1`.

Important new clues:

- `edgesynology1` logs show Snapshot Replication tasks were created on `2026-05-29 10:53:15`:
  - `Share [files] Snapshot`
  - `Share [mac] Snapshot`
  - `Share [styleguides] Snapshot`

- `edgesynology2` logs show hourly scheduler failures at exactly `:00:01`, matching the earlier iowait/snapshot timing pattern:
  - `Failed to parse json string`
  - `SYNOSchedNotifySettingGet failed`
  - repeated at `13:00`, `14:00`, `15:00`, etc. on `2026-06-01`

- `edgesynology2` also shows Snapshot/Replication startup activity in scheduler permission logs around `03:55-03:56`, including:
  - `iscsi_snapshot_comm_core`
  - `iscsi_snapshot_server`
  - `ReplicationService-synobtrfsreplicad`
  - `synopkgctl start SnapshotReplication`

Storage detail improved too:
- It now falls back to `/sys/block/md*/md/*` when `mdadm` is unavailable.
- `edgesynology2` confirms `md0` and `md1` are `degraded=1`, with `raid_disks=6`, while all real data arrays are `degraded=0`.
- SMART health is OK for all listed disks.

Still missing:
- Snapshot schedule/retention rules are not exposed yet.
- `list_snapshot_candidates` still finds no visible snapshots.
- `ReplicationService` DB still has no useful DB files/tables.

Developer next fix: make `check_scheduled_tasks` find the scheduler DB on `edgesynology2`, then add a read-only Snapshot Replication inspector that parses the actual SnapshotReplication schedule/retention config. The logs now strongly suggest the interesting events are hourly scheduled snapshot jobs, not scrub.

payload.message:
Yes, this is a meaningful improvement.

The big win: `list_snapshot_candidates` now sees real Btrfs snapshot subvolumes, especially on `edgesynology2`.

On `edgesynology2`, it found many `@sharesnap` snapshots, including:
- `@sharesnap/files/...`
- `@sharesnap/mac/...`
- `@sharesnap/mgmt/...`
- `@sharesnap/users/...`
- `@sharesnap/Coldlion/...`

The timestamps show the snapshot schedule pattern pretty clearly:
- Many shares snapshot at `00:00` local-ish snapshot labels, e.g. `GMT-04-...-20.00.01`.
- `mgmt` snapshots appear around `02:30`.
- `Coldlion` has a lot of weekly/monthly retained snapshots.
- There are old retained snapshots going back to 2023.

That finally confirms backend visibility into the actual snapshot inventory.

Still not fixed:
- `check_scheduled_tasks` still cannot find the scheduler DB on `edgesynology2`.
- It checked more paths this time, but still not the right one. `edgesynology1` has `/host/usr/syno/etc/esynoscheduler/esynoscheduler.db`; `edgesynology2` still only reports candidate roots like `/host/etc`, `/host/packages`, `/etc`, `/var/packages`.
- DSM WebAPI task-list fallback still fails with `{"error":{"code":103},"success":false}`.
- `fetch_package_db` for both `SnapshotReplication` and `ReplicationService` still finds no DB files/tables.

New useful observation: `edgesynology2` scheduler logs continue to show hourly failures through this morning, June 2, 2026:
- `Failed to parse json string`
- `SYNOSchedNotifySettingGet failed`
- at `00:00`, `01:00`, `02:00`, `03:00`, `03:30`, `04:00`, `04:36`, `05:00`, `06:00`, `07:00`, `08:00`, `09:00`.

So: snapshot inventory is now visible, but schedule/retention config is not yet. The next developer target is still scheduler DB discovery/API auth on `edgesynology2`, plus a snapshot summary tool that groups snapshots by share and creation time so we can see retention density without reading giant raw lists.

payload.message:
After the rebuild: yes, much better. The key gap is now fixed.

`edgesynology2` now exposes the scheduler DB:

```text
/host/usr/syno/etc/esynoscheduler/esynoscheduler.db
/usr/syno/etc/esynoscheduler/esynoscheduler.db
```

It also now gets a successful read-only DSM WebAPI task list. That gives us the snapshot schedule view we were missing.

**edgesynology2 Snapshot Tasks**
Current read-only task list shows:

```text
Share [mac] Snapshot          next: 2026-06-02 11:00 enabled
Share [Photography] Snapshot  next: 2026-06-02 11:00 enabled
Share [users] Snapshot        next: 2026-06-02 11:00 enabled
Share [mgmt] Snapshot         next: 2026-06-02 11:00 enabled
Share [files] Snapshot        next: 2026-06-02 13:00 enabled
Share [Coldlion] Snapshot     next: 2026-06-03 08:00 enabled
Share [styleguides] Snapshot  next: 2026-06-05 16:00 disabled
```

That confirms the earlier suspicion: several snapshot tasks are clustered at the same time. Four shares fire together at `11:00`, which is exactly the kind of thing that can create Btrfs metadata churn and iowait spikes.

`list_snapshot_candidates` still works and shows real `@sharesnap/...` snapshots on `edgesynology2`, including old retained snapshots for `Coldlion`, `files`, `mgmt`, `mac`, and `users`.

**Current Health**
- Both NAS APIs are reachable.
- Loads are moderate: both around `1.2-1.3`.
- `edgesynology1`: no D-state waiters, disk queues quiet.
- `edgesynology2`: one transient D-state `[awk]` process and active queue on `md2`/`dm-2`, but not the severe multi-worker Btrfs/RAID pileup we saw earlier.
- `edgesynology2` still has `md0/md1 degraded=1` because those system RAID1 arrays expect 6 disks and have 5 present. Data arrays are clean/degraded=0.
- SMART detail on `edgesynology2` did not print health lines this time, but data-array state is clean.

The next practical step is to stagger those `edgesynology2` snapshot jobs. I’d move `mac`, `Photography`, `users`, and `mgmt` off the same `11:00` slot, maybe `11:00`, `11:20`, `11:40`, `12:00`, and keep `files` at `13:00` or move it farther away if it’s large.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

TOOL: disable_scheduled_task
GROUP: write_tasks

TOOL: find_path_versions_and_snapshots
GROUP: files
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Looks for recoverable versions of a path: Btrfs snapshots under @Recently-Snapshot, recycle bin entries, and any Drive version hints in logs. Requires exact_path pointing to a file or directory.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  exact_path: string  // Exact absolute filesystem path to inspect (e.g. /volume1/data/folder/file.txt).

TOOL: inspect_snapshot_replication
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Read-only Snapshot Replication inspection with DSM 7 config/DB discovery: package state, daemon runtime, candidate schedule/retention config files, SQLite tables, snapshot counts, and recent prune/delete events. Does not start/cancel scrubs, create/delete snapshots, or run DSM tasks.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: list_snapshot_candidates
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Lists available Btrfs snapshots on all volumes that could be used for path recovery. Shows snapshot names and recent snapshot directories without recursively scanning snapshot sizes. Optionally pass a name fragment in filter to narrow results.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: restore_path_from_snapshot
GROUP: write_files
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Restores a file or directory from a Btrfs snapshot. Pass the snapshot path (from list_snapshot_candidates) and the destination path in filter as 'snapshot_path|dest_path'. Both must be absolute. Does NOT overwrite — destination must not exist.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: summarize_snapshots_by_share
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Summarizes visible Btrfs snapshots by share and creation-time bucket without dumping giant raw lists. Use to inspect Snapshot Replication retention density per shared folder. Optional filter narrows to a share/path fragment.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: get_resource_snapshot
GROUP: performance
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Full live picture of the NAS: top processes by CPU/memory, disk I/O per disk, active SMB/NFS/Drive connections, ShareSync backlog, memory pressure, and recent kernel errors. Best first tool to run when diagnosing any performance problem.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: collect_incident_bundle
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Collects a targeted set of diagnostics for a specific incident type. Pass the type in filter: 'drive' (Drive/ShareSync), 'storage' (RAID/SMART/Btrfs), 'network' (interfaces/DNS), 'permission' (ACL/share), or 'crash' (daemon/OOM/segfault). Omit filter for general system snapshot.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: cancel_btrfs_scrub
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Cancels an in-progress Btrfs scrub on a volume. Pass the volume name (e.g. 'volume1') in filter. Use check_scrub_status first to confirm a scrub is running.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: cancel_smart_test
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Cancels an in-progress SMART self-test on a specific disk. Pass the device name (e.g. 'sda') in filter. Use check_smart_test_progress first to confirm a test is running.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: check_btrfs_detail
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows Btrfs filesystem usage, device error counters, balance status, and subvolume list for all Btrfs data volumes. Use to diagnose Btrfs-specific errors or unexpected space allocation.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_disk_error_trends
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows SMART error-relevant attributes (reallocated sectors, pending sectors, uncorrectable errors, temperature, power-on hours) for all disks in a compact table. Use to spot a drive silently accumulating errors.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_filesystem_health
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Checks filesystem mount status, inode usage, RAID status, and SMART disk health across all active volumes.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_scrub_status
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows Btrfs scrub status and history for all data volumes, and RAID sync/check progress from /proc/mdstat. Use to see if a scrub is running or when the last one completed.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_smart_detail
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows full SMART attributes, error log, and self-test history for a disk. Specify a device in filter (e.g. /dev/sda) or leave empty to check all disks.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: check_smart_test_progress
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows the current progress, remaining time, and recent log of active SMART self-tests. Pass a device in filter (e.g. /dev/sda) to check a specific disk, or leave empty to check all disks.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: check_storage_pool_detail
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows detailed storage pool and RAID array state using discovered DSM binaries where present, with read-only fallbacks to mdstat, sysfs MD state, and SMART health.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_volume_quota_and_inode_pressure
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows inode usage pressure and Btrfs qgroup quota state for all data volumes. High inode usage can prevent new files from being created even when disk space is available.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: fetch_package_db
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Queries a named DSM package's SQLite database in read-only mode. Pass the package name and optionally a read-only SQL query in filter (SELECT/WITH or safe schema PRAGMA only). Omit filter to list tables. Use for deep investigation of Drive, HyperBackup, or scheduler internal state.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  package_name: string  // Synology package name exactly as shown by synopkg (e.g. SynologyDrive, HyperBackup, ContainerManager).
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: inspect_recycle_bin
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Lists contents of the Synology recycle bin across all volumes and shares. Pass a filename or path fragment in filter to search for a specific file. Shows timestamps, sizes, and full paths.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: list_drive_version_history
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Queries the Synology Drive SQLite database for version history of a specific file. Requires exact_path. Shows available version tables and attempts to list versions by filename.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  exact_path: string  // Exact absolute filesystem path to inspect (e.g. /volume1/data/folder/file.txt).

TOOL: persist_vm_overcommit_memory
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Persists the vm.overcommit_memory sysctl setting across reboots by writing to /etc/sysctl.conf on the NAS host. Run after set_vm_overcommit_memory to make the change survive a reboot. Pass value in filter (default: 1).
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: set_io_scheduler
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Changes the I/O scheduler for a block device. mq-deadline is recommended for HDDs and RAID arrays (reduces seek storms, prioritises latency). none or mq-deadline for SSDs. Pass filter as 'device scheduler', e.g. 'sda mq-deadline' or 'md0 mq-deadline'. Not persistent across reboots.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: set_ionice
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Changes a process's I/O scheduling class via ionice. Allowed classes: 2=best-effort (normal; pass optional priority 0-7 where 0=highest, 7=lowest), 3=idle (runs I/O only when no other process needs the disk). Class 1 (realtime) is not permitted. Use to deprioritize a heavy background process (indexer, backup daemon) crowding out foreground I/O. Pass filter as 'PID class [priority]', e.g. '1234 3' or '1234 2 6'.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: set_vm_dirty_ratios
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Tunes vm.dirty_ratio and vm.dirty_background_ratio via sysctl. Lowering these forces dirty pages to flush more aggressively, smoothing out iowait spikes from large write bursts. Default Linux values: dirty_ratio=20 dirty_background_ratio=10. Recommended for NAS: 5 and 3. Pass as 'dirty_ratio=N dirty_background_ratio=M' in filter. Not persistent across reboots.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: set_vm_overcommit_memory
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Sets vm.overcommit_memory via sysctl. Use value 1 to allow overcommit (fixes invoked/synologand OOM conditions). Change is live immediately but not persistent across reboots. Pass value (0, 1, or 2) in filter.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: start_btrfs_scrub
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Starts a Btrfs scrub on a volume to check data integrity. Pass the volume name (e.g. 'volume1') in filter, or leave empty to start scrub on all Btrfs volumes. Scrubs run in the background; use check_scrub_status to monitor progress.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: start_smart_test
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Starts a SMART self-test on a specific disk. Pass test type and device in filter as 'short:/dev/sda' or 'long:/dev/sda'. Use check_smart_detail to see results after the test completes (short: ~2min, long: ~hours).
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

Default Linux values: dirty_ratio=20 dirty_background_ratio=10. Recommended for NAS: 5 and 3. Pass as 'dirty_ratio=N dirty_background_ratio=M' in filter. Not persistent across reboots.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: set_vm_overcommit_memory
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Sets vm.overcommit_memory via sysctl. Use value 1 to allow overcommit (fixes invoked/synologand OOM conditions). Change is live immediately but not persistent across reboots. Pass value (0, 1, or 2) in filter.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: start_btrfs_scrub
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Starts a Btrfs scrub on a volume to check data integrity. Pass the volume name (e.g. 'volume1') in filter, or leave empty to start scrub on all Btrfs volumes. Scrubs run in the background; use check_scrub_status to monitor progress.

  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: start_smart_test

DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Starts a SMART self-test on a specific disk. Pass test type and device in filter as 'short:/dev/sda' or 'long:/dev/sda'. Use check_smart_detail to see results after the test completes (short: ~2min, long: ~hours).

PARAMS:

TOOL: check_backup_status
GROUP: backup
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Checks Hyper Backup package status, lists backup tasks, and shows recent backup log entries — especially errors, failures, and destination connectivity issues. Discovers backup log files across every known candidate path (DSM 6, DSM 7, per-task dirs) and tails the freshest one, so it works even when the standard log path is stale or empty.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.

TOOL: check_io_stalls
GROUP: performance
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Looks for processes stuck waiting on disk (D-state), measures I/O wait percentage, checks disk queue depth, RAID sync/rebuild status, and finds hung task kernel warnings. Run when the NAS feels slow or unresponsive.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_psi_pressure
GROUP: performance
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Reads Linux PSI (Pressure Stall Information) — the most precise modern metric for resource contention. Reports what % of time tasks were stalled waiting for I/O, CPU, or memory. 'some' = at least one task stalled; 'full' = all runnable tasks stalled (device fully saturated). Any 'full io' value above 5% indicates serious I/O saturation. Available on DSM 7.x (kernel 4.20+).
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_sharesync_status
GROUP: drive_sync
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Looks specifically for stuck, conflicted, or erroring ShareSync tasks in the recent logs. Searches across all data volumes.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.

PARAMS:

- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Thread Coordination
- When the user asks to create, inspect, continue, pin, archive, rename, or otherwise manage Codex threads in the background, search for the relevant thread tool first: `create_thread`, `list_threads`, `read_thread`, `send_message_to_thread`, `set_thread_pinned`, `set_thread_archived`, or `set_thread_title`.

### Inline Code Comments
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive when you need to attach feedback directly to specific code lines.
- Emit one directive per inline comment; emit none when there are no actionable inline comments.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3).
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

payload.message: The search index is being literal there, so I’m widening it. I’m looking specifically for read-only snapshot inventory and any write-side scheduler tool; the distinction matters because I don’t want to fake a reschedule by poking DSM internals.

content.text: The search index is being literal there, so I’m widening it. I’m looking specifically for read-only snapshot inventory and any write-side scheduler tool; the distinction matters because I don’t want to fake a reschedule by poking DSM internals.

Found 28 tool(s) for "snapshot". Showing top 20.
Execute any of these with: invoke_tool({ name: "<tool>", target: "edgesynology1|edgesynology2|both", args: { ... } })

TOOL: create_prechange_snapshot
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Creates a read-only Btrfs snapshot of a volume as a recovery point before making changes. Pass the volume path (e.g. /volume1) in filter, or leave empty to snapshot all Btrfs volumes. Snapshot is named @prechange_{timestamp}.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: find_path_versions_and_snapshots
GROUP: files
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Looks for recoverable versions of a path: Btrfs snapshots under @Recently-Snapshot, recycle bin entries, and any Drive version hints in logs. Requires exact_path pointing to a file or directory.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  exact_path: string  // Exact absolute filesystem path to inspect (e.g. /volume1/data/folder/file.txt).

TOOL: inspect_snapshot_replication
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Read-only Snapshot Replication inspection with DSM 7 config/DB discovery: package state, daemon runtime, candidate schedule/retention config files, SQLite tables, snapshot counts, and recent prune/delete events. Does not start/cancel scrubs, create/delete snapshots, or run DSM tasks.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: list_snapshot_candidates
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Lists available Btrfs snapshots on all volumes that could be used for path recovery. Shows snapshot names and recent snapshot directories without recursively scanning snapshot sizes. Optionally pass a name fragment in filter to narrow results.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: restore_path_from_snapshot
GROUP: write_files
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Restores a file or directory from a Btrfs snapshot. Pass the snapshot path (from list_snapshot_candidates) and the destination path in filter as 'snapshot_path|dest_path'. Both must be absolute. Does NOT overwrite — destination must not exist.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: summarize_snapshots_by_share
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Summarizes visible Btrfs snapshots by share and creation-time bucket without dumping giant raw lists. Use to inspect Snapshot Replication retention density per shared folder. Optional filter narrows to a share/path fragment.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: get_resource_snapshot
GROUP: performance
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Full live picture of the NAS: top processes by CPU/memory, disk I/O per disk, active SMB/NFS/Drive connections, ShareSync backlog, memory pressure, and recent kernel errors. Best first tool to run when diagnosing any performance problem.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: collect_incident_bundle
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Collects a targeted set of diagnostics for a specific incident type. Pass the type in filter: 'drive' (Drive/ShareSync), 'storage' (RAID/SMART/Btrfs), 'network' (interfaces/DNS), 'permission' (ACL/share), or 'crash' (daemon/OOM/segfault). Omit filter for general system snapshot.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: cancel_btrfs_scrub
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Cancels an in-progress Btrfs scrub on a volume. Pass the volume name (e.g. 'volume1') in filter. Use check_scrub_status first to confirm a scrub is running.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: cancel_smart_test
GROUP: write_storage
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Cancels an in-progress SMART self-test on a specific disk. Pass the device name (e.g. 'sda') in filter. Use check_smart_test_progress first to confirm a test is running.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: check_btrfs_detail
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows Btrfs filesystem usage, device error counters, balance status, and subvolume list for all Btrfs data volumes. Use to diagnose Btrfs-specific errors or unexpected space allocation.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_disk_error_trends
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows SMART error-relevant attributes (reallocated sectors, pending sectors, uncorrectable errors, temperature, power-on hours) for all disks in a compact table. Use to spot a drive silently accumulating errors.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_filesystem_health
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Checks filesystem mount status, inode usage, RAID status, and SMART disk health across all active volumes.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_scrub_status
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows Btrfs scrub status and history for all data volumes, and RAID sync/check progress from /proc/mdstat. Use to see if a scrub is running or when the last one completed.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_smart_detail
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows full SMART attributes, error log, and self-test history for a disk. Specify a device in filter (e.g. /dev/sda) or leave empty to check all disks.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: check_smart_test_progress
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows the current progress, remaining time, and recent log of active SMART self-tests. Pass a device in filter (e.g. /dev/sda) to check a specific disk, or leave empty to check all disks.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: check_storage_pool_detail
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows detailed storage pool and RAID array state using discovered DSM binaries where present, with read-only fallbacks to mdstat, sysfs MD state, and SMART health.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: check_volume_quota_and_inode_pressure
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Shows inode usage pressure and Btrfs qgroup quota state for all data volumes. High inode usage can prevent new files from being created even when disk space is available.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.

TOOL: fetch_package_db
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Queries a named DSM package's SQLite database in read-only mode. Pass the package name and optionally a read-only SQL query in filter (SELECT/WITH or safe schema PRAGMA only). Omit filter to list tables. Use for deep investigation of Drive, HyperBackup, or scheduler internal state.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  package_name: string  // Synology package name exactly as shown by synopkg (e.g. SynologyDrive, HyperBackup, ContainerManager).
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: inspect_recycle_bin
GROUP: recovery
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Lists contents of the Synology recycle bin across all volumes and shares. Pass a filename or path fragment in filter to search for a specific file. Shows timestamps, sizes, and full paths.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

(8 additional match(es) not shown — refine the query or raise limit.)

content.text:
Found 5 tool(s) for "write_tasks". Showing top 5.
Execute any of these with: invoke_tool({ name: "<tool>", target: "edgesynology1|edgesynology2|both", args: { ... } })

TOOL: disable_scheduled_task
GROUP: write_tasks
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Disables a DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI. Use to suppress a runaway or broken task without deleting it. Pass the task ID (integer from check_scheduled_tasks) in filter. Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: enable_scheduled_task
GROUP: write_tasks
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Enables a disabled DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI. Pass the task ID (integer from check_scheduled_tasks) in filter. Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: generate_support_bundle
GROUP: write_tasks
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Generates a DSM support bundle (synosupportd or synologand support-collect) and saves it to /tmp. Returns the path so it can be retrieved with fetch_support_artifacts or fetch_log_file.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: run_scheduled_task
GROUP: write_tasks
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Triggers an immediate run of a DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI. Pass the task ID (integer from check_scheduled_tasks) in filter. Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: trigger_backup_task
GROUP: write_tasks
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Triggers an immediate run of a HyperBackup task via DSM WebAPI. Pass the task ID (integer from check_backup_status) in filter. Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

Found 7 tool(s) for "task schedule". Showing top 7.
Execute any of these with: invoke_tool({ name: "<tool>", target: "edgesynology1|edgesynology2|both", args: { ... } })

TOOL: check_scheduled_tasks
GROUP: system
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Lists DSM scheduled task metadata and recent scheduler errors using DSM 7 path discovery. SQLite databases are opened read-only; missing paths are reported with the candidate list that was checked.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  lookback_hours: number (optional, default=2)  // How many hours of logs to look back. Default: 2.

TOOL: disable_scheduled_task
GROUP: write_tasks
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Disables a DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI. Use to suppress a runaway or broken task without deleting it. Pass the task ID (integer from check_scheduled_tasks) in filter. Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: enable_scheduled_task
GROUP: write_tasks
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Enables a disabled DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI. Pass the task ID (integer from check_scheduled_tasks) in filter. Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: run_scheduled_task
GROUP: write_tasks
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Triggers an immediate run of a DSM scheduled task via DSM WebAPI. Pass the task ID (integer from check_scheduled_tasks) in filter. Requires DSM_USERNAME/DSM_PASSWORD in .env. Shows a preview and asks for your approval before doing anything.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: restart_scheduler_services
GROUP: write_restart
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Restarts crond. Use when scheduled tasks have stopped running and restart of invoked alone did not resolve it. DSM respawns crond automatically.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

TOOL: inspect_snapshot_replication
GROUP: storage
TYPE: read
DESCRIPTION: Read-only Snapshot Replication inspection with DSM 7 config/DB discovery: package state, daemon runtime, candidate schedule/retention config files, SQLite tables, snapshot counts, and recent prune/delete events. Does not start/cancel scrubs, create/delete snapshots, or run DSM tasks.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  filter: string (optional)  // Search term, file path, or folder name depending on the tool.

TOOL: restart_invoked_related_services
GROUP: write_restart
TYPE: write (requires confirmed: true to execute)
DESCRIPTION: WRITE — Restarts the invoked daemon and esynoscheduler (DSM task scheduler). Use when scheduled tasks are not running or invoked is hung. DSM will respawn after kill.
PARAMS:
  target: "edgesynology1" | "edgesynology2" | "both"  // Which NAS to run on. Use 'both' to query both simultaneously.
  confirmed: boolean (optional, default=false — omit to preview, set true to execute)

payload.message:
I can inspect it now, but I still **cannot safely reschedule Snapshot Replication through the current MCP toolset**.

What changed after the rebuild:

- Snapshot inventory works. `summarize_snapshots_by_share` can see backend `@sharesnap` data.
- On `edgesynology2`, it confirms heavy Snapshot Replication activity:
  - `Photography`: 30 snapshots, newest `2026.06.02-10`
  - `files`: 164 snapshots, newest `2026.06.02-10`
  - `mac`: 50 snapshots, newest `2026.06.02-10`
  - `mgmt`: 372 snapshots, newest `2026.06.02-08`
  - `users`: 48 snapshots, newest `2026.06.02-10`
  - `Coldlion`: 63 snapshots, newest `2026.06.02-08`
- This matches the earlier iowait story: multiple shares firing/pruning close together, especially on `edgesynology2`.

But the MCP write tools still only expose:

- enable scheduled task
- disable scheduled task
- run scheduled task
- restart scheduler/invoked services

There is **no edit/update schedule tool** exposed yet. So I can’t move `Share [Photography] Snapshot` from `11:00` to `11:20` via MCP without doing something hacky like editing DSM scheduler databases directly, which I would not recommend.

The schedule I’d apply on `edgesynology2` is:

```text
Share [mac] Snapshot           id 5   11:00
Share [Photography] Snapshot   id 21  11:20
Share [users] Snapshot         id 12  11:40
Share [mgmt] Snapshot          id 15  12:00
Share [files] Snapshot         id 4   13:00 or 13:20
Share [Coldlion] Snapshot      id 8   leave at 08:00 unless it overlaps pain
```

Also: the new `inspect_snapshot_replication` tool itself currently fails with `command exceeds 4096 bytes` on both NAS units. That’s a developer-side MCP packaging issue: the generated read-only probe command is too large for the NAS API command-size limit. The developer should split that probe into smaller commands or run a compact script already present inside the container/NAS API tier.

);

);

payload.message:
Yes, better now. The rebuilt MCP can inspect Snapshot Replication backend successfully.

Key result: `edgesynology2` snapshot jobs are now visibly staggered in DSM’s scheduler:

```text
Share [mac] Snapshot           id 5   next 2026-06-02 11:22
Share [users] Snapshot         id 12  next 2026-06-02 11:52
Share [mgmt] Snapshot          id 15  next 2026-06-02 12:08
Share [files] Snapshot         id 4   next 2026-06-02 13:00
Share [Photography] Snapshot   id 21  next 2026-06-02 13:35
Share [Coldlion] Snapshot      id 8   next 2026-06-03 08:00
```

That is much healthier than the previous pileup around `11:00`. The backend also confirms these tasks are Snapshot Replication / Shared Folder tasks and DSM reports `can_edit: false` through `SYNO.Core.TaskScheduler`, which means editing them via the generic Task Scheduler API still is not exposed. The schedule change likely has to go through Snapshot Replication’s own schedule API, not the Task Scheduler edit path.

The new inspection also confirms the scale difference:

```text
edgesynology1: /btrfs/volume1 btrfs_snapshots=113
edgesynology2: /btrfs/volume1 btrfs_snapshots=1349
```

So `edgesynology2` is still the one with much heavier snapshot metadata load.

One remaining MCP-side issue: the tool output is getting JSON lines truncated/wrapped in places, but the probe itself is no longer blocked. For the developer, the next useful write tool would be something like `set_snapshot_schedule`, implemented against the Snapshot Replication / `SYNO.Core.Share.Snapshot` schedule API, not generic Task Scheduler.
